steps.title: Introduction to the Variable Inspector
steps.content: The Variable Inspector is a powerful debugging tool that automatically tracks variables during code execution. It's located in the Debug tab, below the main debug output area.

steps.title: How Variable Tracking Works
steps.content: The Variable Inspector automatically detects variables from your debug messages. When you add debug messages with variable assignments, the system will track them automatically. Let's see this in action.

steps.title: Adding Debug Statements
steps.content: Let's modify our solution for Problem 1 to include debug statements. We'll include some variables to track in our debug messages.

steps.title: Running the Code
steps.content: Now let's run the code to see the Variable Inspector in action. Make sure debug output is enabled by checking the 'Enable Debug' checkbox in the Debug tab.

steps.title: Execute the Solution
steps.content: Click the Run Code button to execute the solution. The Variable Inspector will automatically track the variables 'i' and 'total' as the code runs.

steps.title: Examining Tracked Variables
steps.content: After the code runs, you can see the tracked variables in the Variable Inspector panel. Notice how it shows the variable name, current value, and when it was last updated.

steps.title: Variable Inspector Features
steps.content:
The Variable Inspector offers several useful features:
• Variables are color-coded by debug level
• Timestamps show when variables were updated
• Values are automatically updated when they change
• You can search for specific variables using the search box

steps.title: Context Menu Options
steps.content:
You can right-click on the Variable Inspector to access additional options:
• Copy: Copy selected variable details
• Select All: Select all variables
• Clear Variables: Remove all tracked variables

steps.title: Tracking Different Variables
steps.content: Different debug levels are represented by different colors. Let's modify our code to use different debug levels for better organization.

steps.title: Filtering Debug Levels
steps.content: You can filter which debug levels are displayed by toggling the checkboxes at the top of the Debug panel. Try unchecking 'Debug' to hide regular debug messages.

steps.title: Using the Search Feature
steps.content: You can search for specific variables or values using the search box. Try typing 'total' to highlight all occurrences in both debug output and the Variable Inspector.

steps.title: Using Variable Patterns
steps.content:
The Variable Inspector recognizes two patterns:
• var = value
• var: value

Let's add a variable using the colon format.

params.code:
def solve():
    total = 0
    count = 0
    
    for i in range(1, 1000):
        if i % 3 == 0 or i % 5 == 0:
            total += i
            count += 1
            debug(f"i = {i}", "debug")
            debug(f"total = {total}", "info")
            debug(f"count: {count}", "info")
    
    return total

steps.title: Real-world Example
steps.content: The Variable Inspector is especially useful for complex algorithms. Try adding debug statements to track variables in any problem you're solving:

steps.title: Saving Debug Output
steps.content: You can save the debug output (including tracked variables) by clicking the Save button. This allows you to review your debugging session later or share it with others.

steps.title: Clearing Variables
steps.content: You can clear tracked variables at any time by right-clicking on the Variable Inspector and selecting 'Clear Variables'. Variables are also automatically cleared when you switch to a different problem.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.content: Congratulations! You now know how to use the Variable Inspector to track and monitor variables during code execution. This powerful feature will help you debug complex problems and understand how your algorithms work. Remember to use debug messages with variable assignments to get the most out of this tool.
